text: Hello, my name is Alicia Altieri and I'll be presenting a case of robotic liver cyst resection. The patient was an eighty-two year old female who presented with multiple liver cysts. The patient had a history of having two liver cysts resected laparoscopically ten years ago. These new liver cysts were discovered incidentally during a preoperative workup for an orthopedic procedure. Her abnormal EKG prompted an echo which demonstrated an extracardiac structure compressing the right atrium. On CT scan, she was found to have a fifteen centimeter liver cyst. Her symptoms at the time included vague abdominal pain, lower extremity edema, and worsening hypertension. Here are a few slices from her CT scan demonstrating a large posterior liver cyst. The blue arrow identifies the area compressing the heart. As you can see, she has multiple other small cysts throughout the rest of her liver.

The surgical team enters the abdomen using the OptiView technique in the left upper quadrant. The port placement includes three 8mm robotic trocars, a 12mm camera port, and an air sealer device, all positioned in the upper abdomen.

The laparoscopic view reveals multiple small hepatic cysts and extensive adhesions in the right upper quadrant. The surgeon uses electrocautery and blunt dissection to carefully separate the adhesions before docking the robotic system.

The surgeon inserts an aspiration needle laparoscopically into the posterior liver cyst near segments IV-A and VIII, aspirating thick brown fluid. Robotic scissors are used to open the cyst wall, followed by excision using a robotic vessel sealer. The cyst is located adjacent to the falciform ligament and partially attached to the diaphragm.

The surgeon uses the robotic vessel sealer to free the liver cyst laterally from the liver parenchyma, achieving minimal to no bleeding. The cyst is then resected from the diaphragm, with the vessel sealer used to ligate tissue and bluntly dissect the cyst wall free from the diaphragm.

The surgeon uses a robotic suction device to bluntly break apart multiple thin and fragile brown septations within the liver cyst. After aspirating the septations, the cyst cavity is irrigated, revealing the base of the cyst attached to the liver bed. The vessel sealer is used to dissect and resect parts of the cyst, leaving a small amount of cyst wall adherent to the liver bed to avoid injury to major hepatic structures. The area is cleaned with irrigation, and the cavity is inspected for bleeding and bile, appearing hemostatic with no bilious drainage.

The surgeon creates an omental patch using robotic instruments, bringing the omentum from the lower abdomen. The patch, approximately the size of the defect, is placed on the liver cyst bed and secured with a running 3-0 barbed suture, which aids in maintaining hemostasis.

The surgeon uses the robotic vessel sealer as a needle driver, enhancing efficiency by avoiding device exchange. The final product is visible, with the raw surface above identified as the diaphragm. The procedure transitions back to laparoscopy, and a drain is placed in the right upper quadrant. The cyst is placed in an EndoCatch bag and removed through a twelve millimeter trocar, with limited exposure due to the angled laparoscopic camera.

The fascia of the robotic port sites was closed with the Carter-Thompson device. Postoperatively, the patient recovered well. After surgery, she was admitted to the step-down unit. The following day, her diet was advanced and she was discharged to a home with family. On postoperative visit, she was doing well and reported an improvement in her shortness of breath and peripheral edema. Her pathology and cytology were consistent with a benign liver cyst. This was a case of a benign symptomatic liver cyst, which was resected robotically in an eighty-two-year-old female. Thank you for taking the time to watch.
